content.full_text:
CITY COUNCIL

JOHN R. (Randy) SNIDER, Mayor
DAVID M. HINCHMAN
Mayor Pro Tempore
EVELYN M. OLSON
JAMES W. PINKERTON, Jr
FRED M. REID

THOMAS A. PETERSON
City Manager

CITY OF LODI
CITY HALL, 221 WEST PINE STREET
CALL BOX [illegible]
LODI, CALIFORNIA 95241-1910
(209) 334-5634

ALICE M. REIMCHE
City Clerk

BOB McNATT
City Attorney

TELECOPIER (209) 333-6795

November 14, 1990


Deanne Stone, Esquire
Fair Political Practices Commission
State of California
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95812

Subject: REQUEST FOR INFORMAL ADVICE LETTER

The purpose of this letter is to ask that FPPC provide some general
guidance and advice in the matter of Councilmember-elect Phillip Pennino,
based on the following facts:

- Lodi is one of the few cities in California with its own electric
utility. The City's Electric Utility Department supplies electric
power for all industrial, commercial and residential customers inside
the City limits. The annual budget for the Electric Utility
Department this fiscal year totals over $29 million.

- Electric utility rates for City customers are set by the City Council.

- The Electric Utility Department is a regular City department, under
the control of the City Council and City Manager. It has no separate
existence, powers, or board of directors.

- The generating facilities which provide Lodi's electricity are
outside the City. This power is brought to Lodi under an arrangement
known as a "wheeling agreement" via transmission lines owned by
Pacific Gas and Electric Company ("PG&E"). In calendar year 1989,
the City paid PG&E in excess of $1.5 million for these services.

- PG&E serves all areas outside and adjacent to the City, but has no
electric customers inside City limits. In the past, when an area was
annexed to the City, the supplier of electricity changed from PG&E to
the City utility. In some cases, this involved payment from the City
to PG&E for physical improvements in the annexed area.

- PG&E is a major corporation whose stock is traded on the New York
Stock Exchange.

- Mr. Pennino is employed full time by PG&E as an Economic Development
Coordinator. He also owns PG&E stock worth in excess of $10,000.

Deanne Stone, Esquire
Fair Political Practices Commission
November 14, 1990
Page Two


This situation brings to mind a number of questions such as:

1)	Can Mr. Pennino take part in actions involving agreements between
	PG&E and the City of Lodi?

2)	Can he properly vote on the portion of the City budget for the
	Electric Utility Department, where part of that budget includes
	payments to PG&E for "wheeling"?

3)	Can Mr. Pennino properly vote on the remainder of the City's annual
	budget (that portion other than for the Electric Utility Department)?

4)	Can Mr. Pennino properly vote on establishing or setting electric
	utility rates for customers inside the City limits?

5)	Can Mr. Pennino properly vote on annexations of land to the City of
	Lodi where the subject property is presently served by PG&E but
	would, following annexation, be served by the City's electric utility?

Your assistance in this matter is deeply appreciated. If further facts are
necessary, please give me a call at (209) 333-6701.

Sincerely yours,


BOB W. McNATT
City Attorney

BM:vc

cc:	Lodi City Council Members
	Lodi City Council Members-elect
	City Manager

COI3/TXTA.01V

sections.question:
This situation brings to mind a number of questions such as:
1) Can Mr. Pennino take part in actions involving agreements between PG&E and the City of Lodi?
2) Can he properly vote on the portion of the City budget for the Electric Utility Department, where part of that budget includes payments to PG&E for "wheeling"?
3) Can Mr. Pennino properly vote on the remainder of the City's annual budget (that portion other than for the Electric Utility Department)?
4) Can Mr. Pennino properly vote on establishing or setting electric utility rates for customers inside the City limits?
5) Can Mr. Pennino properly vote on annexations of land to the City of Lodi where the subject property is presently served by PG&E but would, following annexation, be served by the City's electric utility?
sections.question_synthetic: The City of Lodi requests guidance on whether Councilmember-elect Phillip Pennino, who is employed by PG&E and owns PG&E stock worth over $10,000, can vote on various city matters involving the electric utility budget, rates, agreements with PG&E, and annexations.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from a city attorney to FPPC, not an FPPC response. No conclusion section exists. The document clearly identifies itself as a 'REQUEST FOR INFORMAL ADVICE LETTER' and presents five specific questions about voting eligibility and conflict of interest issues.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: This situation brings to mind a number of questions such as:
1) Can Mr. Pennino take part in actions involving agreements between PG&E and the City of Lodi?
2) Can he properly vote on the portion of the City budget for the Electric Utility Department, where part of that budget includes payments to PG&E for "wheeling"?
3) Can Mr. Pennino properly vote on the remainder of the City's annual budget (that portion other than for the Electric Utility Department)?
4) Can Mr. Pennino properly vote on establishing or setting electric utility rates for customers inside the City limits?
5) Can Mr. Pennino properly vote on annexations of land to the City of Lodi where the subject property is presently served by PG&E but would, following annexation, be served by the City's electric utility?

embedding.summary: This is an incoming request from the City of Lodi's City Attorney to the FPPC seeking informal advice regarding conflict of interest issues for Councilmember-elect Phillip Pennino, who has employment and financial interests with PG&E, a company with significant business relationships with the City.
